title: Gene set analysis and GRN inference on PBMCs
text: vignette demonstrates gene set scoring, spatial autocorrelation, gene regulatory network (GRN) inference methods available bixverse. picks PBMC vignette left : QC, normalisation, PCA, nearest neighbours, clustering UMAP assumed done already. read design choices introductory vignette, please first. methods fall two broad categories: first, module scores, AUCell, VISION, takes pre-defined gene sets scores per cell. second, Hotspot SCENIC, discovers gene programmes regulatory relationships data . use PBMC3k dataset throughout. 2,700 cells likely small GRN methods produce biologically meaningful results, large enough show API workflow end end. real datasets tens thousands cells code applies unchanged. Also, Note vignette built GitHub runner 2-core machine ~8 GB memory. works incredible enough, take account looking vignette. reconstruct processed SingleCells object PBMC vignette. chunk hidden brevity: downloads data, runs QC, HVG selection, PCA, neighbour computation, Leiden clustering UMAP.
code: library(bixverse) library(ggplot2) library(data.table) library(magrittr) pbmc3k_path <- bixverse:::download_pbmc3k() tempdir_pbmc <- tempdir()  sc_object <- SingleCells(dir_data = tempdir_pbmc) mtx_io_params <- get_cell_ranger_params(pbmc3k_path)  sc_object <- load_mtx(   object = sc_object,   sc_mtx_io_param = mtx_io_params,   streaming = FALSE,   .verbose = FALSE )  setnames_sc(sc_object, table = "var", old = "column1", new = "gene_symbol")  var <- get_sc_var(sc_object) ensembl_to_symbol <- setNames(var$gene_symbol, var$gene_id) symbol_to_ensembl <- setNames(var$gene_id, var$gene_symbol)  # gene set proportions for QC gs_of_interest <- list(   MT = var[grepl("^MT-", gene_symbol), gene_id],   Ribo = var[grepl("^RPS|^RPL", gene_symbol), gene_id] ) sc_object <- gene_set_proportions_sc(   sc_object,   gs_of_interest,   streaming = FALSE,   .verbose = FALSE )  # MAD QC qc_df <- sc_object[[c("cell_id", "lib_size", "nnz", "MT")]] metrics <- list(   log10_lib_size = log10(qc_df$lib_size),   log10_nnz = log10(qc_df$nnz),   MT = qc_df$MT ) directions <- c(   log10_lib_size = "twosided",   log10_nnz = "twosided",   MT = "above" ) qc <- run_cell_qc(metrics, directions, threshold = 3) sc_object[["outlier"]] <- qc$combined cells_to_keep <- qc_df[!qc$combined, cell_id] sc_object <- set_cells_to_keep(sc_object, cells_to_keep)  # HVG, PCA, neighbours, clustering, UMAP sc_object <- find_hvg_sc(sc_object, hvg_no = 2000L, .verbose = FALSE) sc_object <- calculate_pca_sc(sc_object, no_pcs = 30L, sparse_svd = TRUE) #> Using sparse SVD solving on scaled data on 2000 HVG. sc_object <- find_neighbours_sc(   sc_object,   neighbours_params = params_sc_neighbours(     knn = list(knn_method = "exhaustive")   ) ) #>  #> Generating sNN graph (full: FALSE). #> Transforming sNN data to igraph. sc_object <- find_clusters_sc(sc_object, res = 1.5, name = "leiden_clusters") sc_object <- umap_sc(sc_object, knn_method = "annoy") #> Running UMAP. #> Using n_epochs = 500 (dataset <10k samples or adam_parallel optimiser) #> Using provided kNN graph.  # UMAP coordinates for reuse umap_dt <- as.data.table(   get_embedding(sc_object, "umap"),   keep.rownames = "cell_id" )

code: pbmc3k_path <- bixverse:::download_pbmc3k() tempdir_pbmc <- tempdir()  sc_object <- SingleCells(dir_data = tempdir_pbmc) mtx_io_params <- get_cell_ranger_params(pbmc3k_path)  sc_object <- load_mtx(   object = sc_object,   sc_mtx_io_param = mtx_io_params,   streaming = FALSE,   .verbose = FALSE )  setnames_sc(sc_object, table = "var", old = "column1", new = "gene_symbol")  var <- get_sc_var(sc_object) ensembl_to_symbol <- setNames(var$gene_symbol, var$gene_id) symbol_to_ensembl <- setNames(var$gene_id, var$gene_symbol)  # gene set proportions for QC gs_of_interest <- list(   MT = var[grepl("^MT-", gene_symbol), gene_id],   Ribo = var[grepl("^RPS|^RPL", gene_symbol), gene_id] ) sc_object <- gene_set_proportions_sc(   sc_object,   gs_of_interest,   streaming = FALSE,   .verbose = FALSE )  # MAD QC qc_df <- sc_object[[c("cell_id", "lib_size", "nnz", "MT")]] metrics <- list(   log10_lib_size = log10(qc_df$lib_size),   log10_nnz = log10(qc_df$nnz),   MT = qc_df$MT ) directions <- c(   log10_lib_size = "twosided",   log10_nnz = "twosided",   MT = "above" ) qc <- run_cell_qc(metrics, directions, threshold = 3) sc_object[["outlier"]] <- qc$combined cells_to_keep <- qc_df[!qc$combined, cell_id] sc_object <- set_cells_to_keep(sc_object, cells_to_keep)  # HVG, PCA, neighbours, clustering, UMAP sc_object <- find_hvg_sc(sc_object, hvg_no = 2000L, .verbose = FALSE) sc_object <- calculate_pca_sc(sc_object, no_pcs = 30L, sparse_svd = TRUE) #> Using sparse SVD solving on scaled data on 2000 HVG. sc_object <- find_neighbours_sc(   sc_object,   neighbours_params = params_sc_neighbours(     knn = list(knn_method = "exhaustive")   ) ) #>  #> Generating sNN graph (full: FALSE). #> Transforming sNN data to igraph. sc_object <- find_clusters_sc(sc_object, res = 1.5, name = "leiden_clusters") sc_object <- umap_sc(sc_object, knn_method = "annoy") #> Running UMAP. #> Using n_epochs = 500 (dataset <10k samples or adam_parallel optimiser) #> Using provided kNN graph.  # UMAP coordinates for reuse umap_dt <- as.data.table(   get_embedding(sc_object, "umap"),   keep.rownames = "cell_id" )

title: Gene set analysis and GRN inference on PBMCs
text: simplest approach gene set activity module score Tirosh et al. (2016): cell, compute mean expression gene set subtract mean expression size-matched control set drawn expression bins. just use lineage-specific genes pretty visualisations.  Module scores lineage genes projected onto UMAP AUCell ranks genes within cell expression computes area recovery curve gene set. robust outliers simple mean. bixverse provides two flavours: Wilcoxon-statistic-based AUC standard AUROC. two highly correlated; show Wilcoxon variant .  AUCell scores lineage genes projected onto UMAP Scoring gene sets useful, natural follow-question : scores spatially structured cell neighbourhood graph, just noise? VISION provides permutation-based test spatial autocorrelation (Geary’s C) kNN graph. Gene sets significant autocorrelation whose activity varies smoothly across cell neighbourhoods – strong indication signal biologically meaningful rather random. VISION expects gene sets signed format pos (optionally) neg components. cause cell lineage genes, use cell type’s markers negatives, just leave blank . Unsurprisingly, gene sets show highly significant spatial correlation.
code: lineage_sets <- list(   `T cell` = symbol_to_ensembl[c(     "CD3D",     "CD3E",     "CD3G",     "CD2",     "IL7R",     "CD7",     "LEF1",     "TCF7",     "LTB",     "TRAC"   )],   `Cytotoxic NK` = symbol_to_ensembl[c(     "NKG7",     "GNLY",     "GZMA",     "GZMB",     "GZMH",     "PRF1",     "CST7",     "KLRD1",     "KLRB1",     "FGFBP2"   )],   `B cell` = symbol_to_ensembl[c(     "CD79A",     "CD79B",     "MS4A1",     "BANK1",     "IGHM",     "IGHD",     "CD74",     "HLA-DQA1",     "TCL1A",     "VPREB3"   )],   `Monocyte` = symbol_to_ensembl[c(     "CD14",     "LYZ",     "S100A8",     "S100A9",     "S100A12",     "CST3",     "FCN1",     "VCAN",     "MNDA",     "TYROBP"   )] )  # drop any NAs lineage_sets <- lapply(lineage_sets, function(x) x[!is.na(x)])  module_scores <- module_scores_sc(   object = sc_object,   gs_list = lineage_sets,   .verbose = FALSE )  ms_dt <- as.data.table(module_scores, keep.rownames = "cell_id") ms_dt <- merge(ms_dt, umap_dt, by = "cell_id")  ms_long <- melt(   ms_dt,   id.vars = c("cell_id", "umap_1", "umap_2"),   measure.vars = names(lineage_sets),   variable.name = "phase",   value.name = "score" )  ggplot(ms_long, aes(x = umap_1, y = umap_2)) +   geom_point(aes(colour = score), size = 0.3) +   scale_colour_viridis_c() +   facet_wrap(~phase, ncol = 2) +   theme_bw() +   labs(colour = "Module\nscore") auc_scores <- aucell_sc(   object = sc_object,   gs_list = lineage_sets,   auc_type = "wilcox",   .verbose = FALSE )  auc_dt <- as.data.table(auc_scores, keep.rownames = "cell_id") auc_dt <- merge(auc_dt, umap_dt, by = "cell_id")  auc_long <- melt(   auc_dt,   id.vars = c("cell_id", "umap_1", "umap_2"),   measure.vars = names(lineage_sets),   variable.name = "phase",   value.name = "auc" )  ggplot(auc_long, aes(x = umap_1, y = umap_2)) +   geom_point(aes(colour = auc), size = 0.3) +   scale_colour_viridis_c() +   facet_wrap(~phase) +   theme_bw() +   labs(colour = "AUC") vision_gs <- lapply(lineage_sets, function(genes) list(pos = genes))  vision_res <- vision_w_autocor_sc(   object = sc_object,   gs_list = vision_gs,   embd_to_use = "pca",   vision_params = params_sc_vision(n_perm = 500L),   .verbose = TRUE ) #> Generating 5 random gene set clusters with a total of 500 permutations.  head(vision_res$auto_cor_dt) #>    gene_set_name  auto_cor       p_val         fdr #>           <char>     <num>       <num>       <num> #> 1:        T cell 0.7567330 0.001996008 0.002661344 #> 2:  Cytotoxic NK 0.4578573 0.009980040 0.009980040 #> 3:        B cell 0.5719954 0.001996008 0.002661344 #> 4:      Monocyte 0.5574494 0.001996008 0.002661344

title: Gene set analysis and GRN inference on PBMCs
text: methods test pre-defined gene sets, Hotspot (DeTomaso & Yosef, 2021) discovers gene programmes de novo testing gene spatial autocorrelation kNN graph grouping significant genes local correlation structure. first step computes Geary’s C every gene neighbour graph. Genes significant autocorrelation whose expression varies smoothly across neighbouring cells, .e., genes mark spatially coherent programmes. Known PBMC markers (LYZ, CD3D, NKG7, etc.) appear among top hits. Taking significant genes forward, compute pairwise local correlations cluster modules. module represents group genes individually autocorrelated also co-vary locally - much stronger signal global correlation alone. returns HotSpot result. Let’s add membership plot .  Let’s pull genes: resulting modules roughly correspond major cell-type programmes PBMCs (T cell, monocyte, B cell, NK cell signatures, etc.). Let’s visualise AUCell check .

<- lapply(hotspot_gene_sets, function(genes) {   list(pos = genes) })  vision_scores_hotspot <- vision_sc(   object = sc_object,   gs_list = hotspot_gene_sets,   .verbose = FALSE )  vision_scores_dt <- as.data.table(   vision_scores_hotspot,   keep.rownames = "cell_id" ) vision_scores_dt <- merge(vision_scores_dt, umap_dt, by = "cell_id")   vision_scores_long <- melt(   vision_scores_dt,   id.vars = c("cell_id", "umap_1", "umap_2"),   measure.vars = names(hotspot_gene_sets),   variable.name = "gene_set",   value.name = "vision_score" )  # min max individually for prettier visualisations vision_scores_long[,   vision_score_scaled := (vision_score - min(vision_score)) /     (max(vision_score) - min(vision_score)),   by = gene_set ]  ggplot(vision_scores_long, aes(x = umap_1, y = umap_2)) +   geom_point(aes(colour = vision_score_scaled), size = 0.3) +   scale_colour_viridis_c() +   facet_wrap(~gene_set) +   theme_bw() +   labs(colour = "Vision (scaled)")

title: Gene set analysis and GRN inference on PBMCs
text: SCENIC infers gene regulatory networks asking: gene, transcription factors (TFs) best predict expression? original implementation uses either random forests GRNBoost2 (gradient boosted trees) regress target gene TFs, extracts feature importances proxy regulatory strength. bixverse implementation re-implements Rust several optimisations make RF ExtraTrees paths substantially faster single-target---time approach used GRNBoost2: Quantisation: TF expression values discretised 256 bins (u8), histogram construction tree building operates single bytes fits comfortably L1 cache. Multi-output batching: 64 target genes share single tree structure. histogram construction cost paid per node per feature, split score aggregates variance reduction across targets batch. amortises dominant cost tree building across targets. Correlated gene batching: rather assigning genes batches randomly, optional SVD + k-means step groups co-expressed genes together shared tree structure informative target batch. GRNBoost2 histogram subtraction: gradient boosted path (single-target), code builds full-feature histograms node derives larger child via parent-minus-smaller subtraction, avoiding redundant scan larger child’s samples. title: Contrastive PCA
text: Standard PCA finds directions maximum variance data. often exactly want — always. many biological settings, dominant sources variance technical contextual: batch effects, cell cycle, sequencing depth, broad signals shared across conditions. swamp subtle biological variation actually care . Contrastive PCA addresses directly. Instead decomposing covariance target data alone, works contrastive covariance matrix: Cα=Σtarget−α⋅ΣbackgroundC_\alpha = \Sigma_{target} - \alpha \cdot \Sigma_{background} Σtarget\Sigma_{target} Σbackground\Sigma_{background} covariance matrices target background datasets, respectively. eigenvectors CαC_\alpha define contrastive principal components — directions capture variance enriched target relative background. parameter α≥0\alpha \geq 0 controls aggressively background signal subtracted. α=0\alpha = 0, contrastive PCA reduces standard PCA target data. α\alpha increases, directions shared background progressively suppressed, components increasingly reflect structure unique target. high α\alpha, risk -subtracting: genuine signal happens partially present background gets removed well. Choosing α\alpha therefore central modelling decision. universally correct value — depends much background signal want remove much target-specific signal remains. typical workflow scan range α\alpha values select one produces meaningful separation target groups, bixverse makes straightforward. One important caveat: contrastive PCA assumes background dataset reasonable proxy unwanted variance. poorly chosen background — one shares little covariance dominant noise target — produce components just standard PCs disguise. Conversely, background similar target -subtract destroy biological signal. method works best principled choice background, healthy controls alongside diseased samples, unlabelled reference population single-cell context.

what: Why do we need yet another single cell framework … ?
title: Technical design choices for the single cell implementation
text: hell write single cell framework scratch use one established libraries? R Seurat, BioConductor universe single cell experiments; Python ScanPy even now GPU-accelerated RAPIDS-singlecell leveraging CUDA Nvidia GPUs. Rust, SingleRust porting ScanPy … hell point, might ask? Well, design philosophy package quite different — whether better worse shall discover, different. Let’s start problem though… quotes recent papers: benchmarked rapids-singlecell 1 million cells 10x Genomics mouse brain dataset, running standard workflow: preprocessing, normalization, HVG selection, dimensionality reduction (PCA, UMAP, t-SNE), neighborhood graph construction, Leiden clustering (Methods). 32-core workstation, pipeline took 52 minutes (Methods) […] — Dicks, et al., arXiv, 2026 instance, typical single-cell data analysis pipeline — encompassing data integration, clustering, visualisation, differential expression analysis — requires 16 h process half million cells standard desktop. cell number slightly increases 600,000, pipeline can crash due memory exceeding, even professional computing platform 512 GB RAM — Li, et al., Nat Comm, 2025 Anyone worked 500k cells can attest painful. Actually painful. question … ? can analytical engines like DuckDB polars handle massive data sets without needing fat memory machines? can easily analyse hundreds millions rows local laptop two. can single cell? answer really “Bro, just throw cores, memory, GPUs problem”? time rethink . Let’s maths. Say single cell experiment 1M cells, average 1000 genes detected per cell. actually happens memory? Worst case: R double precision floats. already looking 40+ GB occupied memory. horrible tibbles 1M rows; throw bunch string columns adding another 250–500 MB nasty situations. Now add R’s copy--modify semantics suddenly everything becomes unbearably slow, memory hungry, end bloated objects storing data rarely actually need memory. question : ? keep raw normalised counts memory ? BPCells going right direction , can even better?  . said. slow let us stop pretending otherwise. Yes, can drop NumPy Rcpp heavy lifting — argument rather proves point! real problem single kernel; happens . lot algorithms practice end looking like : arrow context switch. Data gets passed across language boundary, “simple” bookkeeping happens interpreter — filter , reshape , maybe quick normalisation — hand back . None individual steps looks expensive. Collectively, death thousand cuts: repeated allocations, data copies ask , interpreter’s garbage collector God knows . also lose hope compiler reasoning across boundaries optimising full pipeline. solution make interpreted bits faster. keep data low-level code long possible surface results R user actually needs look something. R becomes thin orchestration layer, active participant computation.

code: /// CsrCellChunk /// /// This structure is designed to store the data of a single cell in a /// CSR-like format optimised for rapid access on disk. #[derive(Debug)] pub struct CsrCellChunk {     pub data_raw: RawCounts,     pub data_norm: Vec<F16>,     pub library_size: usize,     pub indices: Vec<u32>,     pub original_index: usize,     pub to_keep: bool, } /// CscGeneChunk /// /// This structure is designed to store the data of a single gene in a /// CSC-like format optimised for rapid access on disk. #[derive(Encode, Decode, Serialize, Deserialize, Debug)] pub struct CscGeneChunk {     pub data_raw: RawCounts,     pub data_norm: Vec<F16>,     pub avg_exp: F16,     pub nnz: usize,     pub indices: Vec<u32>,     pub original_index: usize,     pub to_keep: bool, }

title: Doublet detection with bixverse
text: Doublets - droplets containing two cells - common artefact droplet-based single cell experiments. left undetected, masquerade intermediate cell states novel populations quietly corrupt downstream analyses. vignette demonstrates two doublet detection approaches available bixverse benchmarks ground truth calls demuxlet, identifies doublets via genetic variation across pooled donors. bixverse provides two computational doublet detection methods: Scrublet simulation-based approach (Wolock, et al., 2019). generates synthetic doublets averaging pairs randomly selected cells, embeds real synthetic cells together, scores real cell proportion nearest neighbours synthetic. bimodal score distribution expected: singlets cluster near zero, doublets near one. threshold score determines final calls. Boosted doublet detection takes different angle. Rather simulating doublets, trains gradient-boosted tree ensemble distinguish real cells synthetic doublets generated way Scrublet. classifier produces probability per cell, doublet calls derived probability. can robust score distribution Scrublet cleanly bimodal, though introduces hyperparameters (slower). methods purely computational work without genetic hashing information. complement experimental approaches like demuxlet, require pooled donors hashtag antibodies. also failure mode large number doublets dominates original data.

title: Doublet detection with bixverse
text: use PBMC data set demuxlet ground truth calls. demuxlet output classifies barcode singlet (SNG) doublet (DBL) (ambiguous, irrelevant situation), giving us something benchmark . Load data bixverse also define small helper compute precision, recall F1 demuxlet calls.
code: doublet_path <- download_demuxlet_pbmc()  tempdir_doublet <- file.path(tempdir(), "demuxlet_bixverse") dir.create(tempdir_doublet, showWarnings = FALSE, recursive = TRUE)  demuxlet_data <- fread(file.path(doublet_path, "demuxlet_calls.tsv"))  table(demuxlet_data$Call) #>  #>   AMB   DBL   SNG  #>    24  1565 13030 sc_object <- SingleCells(dir_data = tempdir_doublet)  mtx_io_params <- get_cell_ranger_params(doublet_path) mtx_io_params$cells_as_rows <- TRUE  sc_object <- load_mtx(   object = sc_object,   sc_mtx_io_param = mtx_io_params ) #> Loading observations data from flat file into the DuckDB. #> Loading variable data from flat file into the DuckDB.  sc_object #> Single cell experiment (Single Cells). #>   No cells (original): 14528 #>    To keep n: 14528 #>   No genes: 12622 #>   HVG calculated: FALSE #>   PCA calculated: FALSE #>   Other embeddings: none #>   KNN generated: FALSE #>   SNN generated: FALSE doublet_metrics <- function(   predicted,   actual,   pos_predicted = TRUE,   pos_actual = "DBL" ) {   tp <- sum(predicted == pos_predicted & actual == pos_actual)   fp <- sum(predicted == pos_predicted & actual != pos_actual)   fn <- sum(predicted != pos_predicted & actual == pos_actual)   precision <- tp / (tp + fp)   recall <- tp / (tp + fn)   f1 <- 2 * (precision * recall) / (precision + recall)   list(precision = precision, recall = recall, f1 = f1) }

title: Doublet detection with bixverse
text: Let’s check two methods see behave plot method shows score distribution observed simulated cells. clean separation two modes indicates threshold sensible.  automatic thresholding perform demuxlet ground truth? automatic threshold always land right place. score distribution suggests different cut-, call_doublets_manual lets override .  made worse , maybe cases scores good. Compared original version, Otsu’s method used identify perfect threshold, might fail situations. boosted approach trains classifier separate real cells synthetic doublets. can work better Scrublet score distribution messy unimodal. However, case running 25 iterations algorithm, means 25x generation synthetic doublets, re-normalisation, PCA, kNN, Louvain, classification. makes slower. using "nndescent", one implemented kNN algorithms can faster data set size. Let’s check boosted works? methods can help identify data-driven way doublets data. example, Scrublet performed better compared boosted doublet detection, see one better data. can worth running compare scores, expected doublet detections, etc. longer idea implement additionally also scDblFinder.
code: scrublet <- scrublet_sc(   object = sc_object,   scrublet_params = params_scrublet(     expected_doublet_rate = 0.1   ) )  scrublet #> ScrubletRes: 14528 cells, 1754 doublets (12.1%) #>   Threshold:              0.1852 #>   Detected doublet rate:  12.1% #>   Detectable fraction:    87.3% #>   Overall doublet rate:   13.8% #>   Simulated doublets:     21792 plot(scrublet) scrublet_dt <- get_obs_data(scrublet) scrublet_dt[, Barcode := get_cell_names(sc_object)] scrublet_dt <- merge(scrublet_dt, demuxlet_data, by = "Barcode")  doublet_metrics(predicted = scrublet_dt$doublet, actual = scrublet_dt$Call) #> $precision #> [1] 0.6641961 #>  #> $recall #> [1] 0.7472739 #>  #> $f1 #> [1] 0.7032901 scrublet_adj <- call_doublets_manual(scrublet, threshold = 0.25) #> Detected doublet rate = 10.0% #> Estimated detectable doublet fraction = 80.4% #> Overall doublet rate: #>   Estimated = 12.4%  plot(scrublet_adj) scrublet_adj_dt <- get_obs_data(scrublet_adj) scrublet_adj_dt[, Barcode := get_cell_names(sc_object)] scrublet_adj_dt <- merge(scrublet_adj_dt, demuxlet_data, by = "Barcode")  doublet_metrics(   predicted = scrublet_adj_dt$doublet,   actual = scrublet_adj_dt$Call ) #> $precision #> [1] 0.7019364 #>  #> $recall #> [1] 0.6510584 #>  #> $f1 #> [1] 0.6755408 boosted_doublets <- doublet_detection_boost_sc(   object = sc_object,   boost_params = params_boost(knn = list(knn_method = "nndescent")) )  boosted_doublets #> BoostRes: 14528 cells, 1243 doublets (8.6%) #>   Score range: [0.0041, 0.7300] boosted_dt <- get_obs_data(boosted_doublets) boosted_dt[, Barcode := get_cell_names(sc_object)] boosted_dt <- merge(boosted_dt, demuxlet_data, by = "Barcode")  doublet_metrics(predicted = boosted_dt$doublet, actual = boosted_dt$Call) #> $precision #> [1] 0.7039421 #>  #> $recall #> [1] 0.5612572 #>  #> $f1 #> [1] 0.6245539

code: library(bixverse) library(magrittr) library(data.table) set.seed(123)  no_nodes <- 100L  barabasi_graph <- igraph::sample_pa(no_nodes, directed = FALSE)  node_names <- sprintf("genes%i", 1:no_nodes)  igraph::V(barabasi_graph)$name <- node_names  # The classes expects a data.table representing the edges # Weighted and directed graphs are supported edge_data <- setDT(igraph::as_data_frame(barabasi_graph))  # We will be creating two diffusion vectors based on the degree distribution # and one set of gold standard genes probs <- igraph::degree(barabasi_graph) / sum(igraph::degree(barabasi_graph))  genes_1 <- sample(   igraph::V(barabasi_graph)$name,   size = 5,   prob = probs ) genes_2 <- sample(   igraph::V(barabasi_graph)$name,   size = 8,   prob = probs )  diffusion_vector_1 <- rep(1, length(genes_1)) %>% `names<-`(genes_1) diffusion_vector_2 <- rep(1, length(genes_2)) %>% `names<-`(genes_2) gold_standard_genes <- sample(   igraph::V(barabasi_graph)$name,   size = 6,   prob = probs ) diffusion_obj <- NetworkDiffusions(   edge_data,   weighted = FALSE,   directed = FALSE ) diffusion_obj <- diffuse_seed_nodes(   object = diffusion_obj,   diffusion_vector = diffusion_vector_1,   summarisation = "max" )  # Should you have duplicated names in the diffusion vector, the function # provides different summarisation methods for the underlying scores diffusion_obj <- permute_seed_nodes(   object = diffusion_obj,   perm_iters = 1000L,   .verbose = FALSE ) diffusion_res <- get_diffusion_vector(diffusion_obj) permutation_res <- get_diffusion_perms(diffusion_obj)  par(mfrow = c(1, 2))  hist(diffusion_res, xlab = "PPR score", main = "Diffusion result") hist(permutation_res, xlab = "Z score", main = "Permutation result") par(mfrow = c(1, 1)) auc_results <- calculate_diffusion_auc(   object = diffusion_obj,   hit_nodes = gold_standard_genes,   permutation_test = TRUE # This will do permutation and generate Z-scores )  print(   sprintf(     "The AUC of the diffuion is %.3f.",     auc_results$auc   ) ) #> [1] "The AUC of the diffuion is 0.589." print(   sprintf(     "The Z-score based on random permutations is %.3f.",     auc_results$z   ) ) #> [1] "The Z-score based on random permutations is 0.764." diffusion_obj <- community_detection(   object = diffusion_obj,   community_params = params_community_detection(     min_nodes = 5L,     threshold_type = "prop_based",     min_seed_nodes = 1L   ) )  results_v1 <- get_results(diffusion_obj) head(results_v1) #>    cluster_id node_id      ks_pval cluster_size seed_nodes_no diffusion_score #>        <char>  <char>        <num>        <int>         <int>           <num> #> 1:  cluster_1  genes1 0.0002750607           11             1 0.0002750607           11             1     0.003384912 #> 5:  cluster_1 genes10 0.0002750607           11             1     0.002746791 #> 6:  cluster_1 genes22 0.0002750607           11             1     0.003231076 #>    seed_node #>       <lgcl> #> 1:     FALSE #> 2:     FALSE #> 3:     FALSE #> 4:     FALSE #> 5:     FALSE #> 6:     FALSE diffusion_obj <- community_detection(   object = diffusion_obj,   community_params = params_community_detection(     min_nodes = 5L,     threshold_type = "pval_based",     pval_threshold = 0.25,     min_seed_nodes = 1L   ) )  results_v2 <- get_results(diffusion_obj) head(results_v2) #>    cluster_id node_id     ks_pval cluster_size seed_nodes_no diffusion_score #>        <char>  <char>       <num>        <int>         <int>           <num> #> 1:  cluster_1  genes9 0.003788689            5             1     0.039472785 #> 2:  cluster_1 genes41 0.003788689            5             1     0.057472209 #> 3:  cluster_1 genes52 0.003788689            5             1     0.024425689 #> 4:  cluster_1 genes78 0.003788689            5             1     0.006710373 #> 5:  cluster_1 genes98 0.003788689            5             1     0.006710373 #>    seed_node #>       <lgcl> #> 1:     FALSE #> 2:      TRUE #> 3:     FALSE #> 4:     FALSE #> 5:     FALSE diffusion_obj <- tied_diffusion(   object = diffusion_obj,   diffusion_vector_1 = diffusion_vector_1,   diffusion_vector_2 = diffusion_vector_2,   summarisation = "max",   score_aggregation = "min" )  # The score aggregation allows to control how the two diffusion results # are combined. # The function will automatically recognise which diffusion was used initially diffusion_obj <- permute_seed_nodes(   object = diffusion_obj,   perm_iters = 1000L,   .verbose = FALSE ) diffusion_res <- get_diffusion_vector(diffusion_obj) permutation_res <- get_diffusion_perms(diffusion_obj)  par(mfrow = c(1, 2))  hist(diffusion_res, xlab = "Tied PPR score", main = "Diffusion result (tied)") hist(   permutation_res,   xlab = "Tied PPR score",   main = "Permutations result (tied)" ) par(mfrow = c(1, 1)) diffusion_obj <- community_detection(   object = diffusion_obj,   community_params = params_community_detection(     min_nodes = 5L,     threshold_type = "prop_based",     min_seed_nodes = 1L   ) )  results_v1 <- get_results(diffusion_obj) head(results_v1) #>    cluster_id node_id      ks_pval cluster_size seed_nodes_1 seed_nodes_2 #>        <char>  <char>        <num>        <int>        <int>        <int> #> 1:  cluster_1  genes5 what: Gene Set Enrichment Methods in bixverse
title: Gene Set Enrichment Methods
text: vignette shows use gene set enrichment methods implemented bixverse.
code: if (!requireNamespace("fgsea", quietly = TRUE)) {   BiocManager::install("fgsea") } if (!requireNamespace("msigdbr", quietly = TRUE)) {   install.packages("msigdbr") } library(bixverse) library(data.table) library(magrittr)

title: Gene Set Enrichment Methods
text: Gene set enrichment analysis sits heart functional genomics workflows. Given set genes interest (ranked list measured genes) want know biological processes, pathways, molecular functions -represented relative expect chance. bixverse provides Rust-accelerated implementations two dominant paradigms , along gene ontology-aware variants . -representation analysis (ORA) via hypergeometric test asks: given drawn kk genes universe NN, KK belong particular gene set, overlap k∩Kk \cap K larger expected? fast interpretable, requires discrete gene list — typically genes passing fold-change significance threshold. choice threshold universe can substantially affect results, known limitation approach. Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) sidesteps working continuously ranked list measured genes, asking whether members gene set systematically concentrated towards top bottom ranking. avoids arbitrary thresholding tends sensitive coordinated modest shifts expression, cost computationally demanding less straightforward interpret. practical complication approaches redundancy Gene Ontology (GO). GO directed acyclic graph: significant hit specific term (e.g., “mitochondrial complex assembly”) typically also produce significant hits ancestor terms (e.g., “oxidative phosphorylation”, “metabolic process”), add new information. bixverse provides two strategies dealing : elimination method Adrian et al., propagates signal DAG removing explained genes, post-hoc simplification approach based Wang semantic similarity, collapses redundant terms running standard enrichment. methods implemented Rust exposed via extendr. performance gains relative existing R implementations range modest (~20% faster GSEA relative fgsea’s Rcpp implementation) substantial: parallelised hypergeometric test large gene set libraries can orders magnitude faster naive R loop.

title: Gene Set Enrichment Methods
text: bixverse implements standard hypergeometric -representation test. example load Hallmark gene sets msigdbr construct named list, expected input format throughout bixverse gene set analyses. test single target gene set, use gse_hypergeometric. gene universe defaults union genes represented gene_set_list provided explicitly. usually reasonable worth checking assay covers non-standard set genes. FDR threshold minimum overlap also configurable. many target gene sets test simultaneously, use gse_hypergeometric_list. hood runs tests parallel via rayon optimiser FxHashSets, making fast even scale. give sense scale handles comfortably, following runs 250 target gene sets 5,000 gene sets drawn universe 20,000 genes — 1.25 million hypergeometric tests total. systems completes seconds.

HALLMARK_INTERFERON_GAMMA_RESPONSE                 45 seed = 10101L  set.seed(seed)  universe <- sprintf("gene_%i", 1:20000) gene_sets_no <- 5000 target_gene_sets_no <- 250  gene_sets <- purrr::map(   1:gene_sets_no,   ~ {     set.seed(seed + .x + 1)     size <- sample(20:100, 1)     sample(universe, size, replace = FALSE)   } )  names(gene_sets) <- purrr::map_chr(   1:gene_sets_no,   ~ { what: Gene Ontology-aware enrichment: the elimination method
title: Gene Set Enrichment Methods
text: naive approach running hypergeometric test independently GO term produces heavily redundant results, significant child terms mechanically inflate ancestors. elimination method Adrian et al. addresses traversing ontology specific terms (leaves) upwards. term reaches significance threshold, genes annotated term removed ancestor terms tests computed. means ancestor terms tested residual signal already explained specific descendants, yielding results less redundant interpretable. bixverse ships human GO data exposes via dedicated S7 class transfers ontology structure Rust efficient traversal. interface mirrors generic hypergeometric functions: single target set list, Rust’s ownership model ensuring ontology structure shared across tests without copying, keeping memory overhead low. version lists: DAG traversal inherently sequential per gene set (level depends one ), tests within level parallelised via rayon. Running 100 target gene sets full human GO completes matter seconds:
code: go_data_dt <- get_go_data_human() #> Loading the data from the package. #> Processing data for the gene_ontology class. #> Warning: The `father` argument of `dfs()` is deprecated as of igraph what: Alternative: post-hoc simplification of GO results
title: Gene Set Enrichment Methods
text: alternative strategy run unconstrained hypergeometric test GO terms collapse redundant results afterwards using semantic similarity. approach flexible — can apply enrichment results, just produced bixverse — adjust test statistics way elimination method . Whether prefer pre-hoc elimination post-hoc simplification depends use case; exploratory analyses simplification approach can convenient, rigorous GO reporting elimination method generally preferable. simplification uses Wang semantic similarity group related terms, retains term best test statistic within cluster. Ties FDR broken ontology depth, preferring specific term.
code: go_data <- load_go_human_data()  min_genes <- 3L  go_genes <- go_data$go_to_genes go_genes_ls <- split(go_genes$ensembl_id, go_genes$go_id) go_genes_ls <- purrr::keep(go_genes_ls, \(x) length(x) > min_genes)  go_results_unfiltered <- gse_hypergeometric(   target_genes = target_genes_1,   gene_set_list = go_genes_ls )  head(go_results_unfiltered) #>    gene_set_name odds_ratios        pvals          fdr  hits gene_set_lengths #>           <char>       <num>        <num>        <num> <num>            <num> #> 1:    GO:0003723   22.120609 1.723343e-23 1.579099e-19    31             1546 #> 2:    GO:0006364   35.837205 2.936032e-12 1.345143e-08    10              159 #> 3:    GO:0005730    8.860092 6.601460e-12 1.581347e-08    23             1927 #> 4:    GO:0042254   43.069954 6.903186e-12 1.581347e-08     9              118 #> 5:    GO:0005634    8.314215 9.730968e-12 1.783297e-08    39             6736 #> 6:    GO:0005654    6.752334 9.279447e-11 1.417126e-07    30             4005 #>    target_set_lengths #>                 <int> #> 1:                 50 #> 2:                 50 #> 3:                 50 #> 4:                 50 #> 5:                 50 #> 6:                 50 go_parent_child_dt <- go_data$gene_ontology[   relationship %in% c("is_a", "part_of") ] %>%   setnames(     old = c("from", "to", "relationship"),     new = c("parent", "child", "type")   )  go_results_simplified <- simplify_hypergeom_res(   res = go_results_unfiltered,   parent_child_dt = go_parent_child_dt,   weights = setNames(c(0.8, 0.6), c("is_a", "part_of")) )  head(go_results_simplified) #>    gene_set_name odds_ratios        pvals          fdr  hits gene_set_lengths #>           <char>       <num>        <num>        <num> <num>            <num> #> 1:    GO:0003723    22.12061 1.723343e-23 1.579099e-19    31             1546 #>    target_set_lengths #>                 <int> #> 1:                 50

title: Gene Set Enrichment Methods
text: bixverse combines elimination method GSEA, using GeneOntologyElim object hypergeometric variant. ontology traversed leaf-first; term permutation-based p-value computed falls elimination threshold, genes term removed ancestors tested. full traversal complete, fgsea multilevel method applied terms reached nominal significance obtain precise p-values pure permutation alone can provide.

title: Ontologies
text: bixverse provides methods computing semantic similarities ontologies — disease ontologies, gene ontologies, phenotype ontologies, . heavy lifting done yet Rust heavy parallelism exploited. makes (usually) faster alternative packages. vignette walks core workflow: building ancestry parent–child table, deriving information content, computing different flavours semantic similarity.

title: Ontologies
text: use Human Phenotype Ontology (HPO) bundled ontologyIndex package. thing bixverse needs data.table three columns: parent, child, type. describing directed edges ontology graph.
code: library("ontologyIndex")  data("hpo")  hpo_data <- as.data.table(stack(hpo$children)) %>%   setnames(old = c("values", "ind"), new = c("child", "parent")) %>%   .[, c("parent", "child"), with = FALSE] %>%   .[, type := "parent_of"]

title: Ontologies
text: computing similarity need two ingredients: full set ancestors every term, information content (IC) derived number descendants term . obtained one step: Armed ancestors IC values can build pairwise similarity matrices entire ontology. bixverse supports three IC-based measures: Resnik similarity — IC informative common ancestor. Lin similarity — Resnik similarity normalised IC two terms. Combined — average (normalised) Resnik Lin scores. matrices rescaled [0,1][0, 1] interval. Note full matrix generations run Vignette generation. fast, GitHub runners memory limited making impossible. Switching Lin similarity just parameter change: Computing full matrix always necessary desirable. care handful terms can calculate pairwise similarities directly, returns long-format data.table instead dense matrix. draw random sample ten terms use combined similarity:
code: c(ancestors, descendants) %<-% get_ontology_ancestry(hpo_data)  information_content <- calculate_information_content(descendants) resnik_similarity <- calculate_semantic_sim_mat(   similarity_type = "resnik",   ancestor_list = ancestors,   ic_list = information_content )  resnik_similarity[1:5, 1:5] lin_similarity <- calculate_semantic_sim_mat(   similarity_type = "lin",   ancestor_list = ancestors,   ic_list = information_content )  lin_similarity[1:5, 1:5] set.seed(10101L)  random_term_sample <- sort(sample(names(information_content), 10L))  combined_similarity <- calculate_semantic_sim(   terms = random_term_sample,   similarity_type = "combined",   ancestor_list = ancestors,   ic_list = information_content )  head(combined_similarity) #>         term1      term2        sims #>        <char>     <char>       <num> #> 1: HP:0000452 HP:0003986 0.003174386 #> 2: HP:0000452 HP:0004921 0.003211837 #> 3: HP:0000452 HP:0009655 0.003211837 #> 4: HP:0000452 HP:0031206 0.003114082 #> 5: HP:0000452 HP:0032411 0.003114082 #> 6: HP:0000452 HP:0034891 0.003114082

title: Ontologies
text: Computing full matrix always necessary desirable. care handful terms can calculate pairwise similarities directly, returns long-format data.table instead dense matrix. draw random sample ten terms use combined similarity:
code: set.seed(10101L)  random_term_sample <- sort(sample(names(information_content), 10L))  combined_similarity <- calculate_semantic_sim(   terms = random_term_sample,   similarity_type = "combined",   ancestor_list = ancestors,   ic_list = information_content )  head(combined_similarity) #>         term1      term2        sims #>        <char>     <char>       <num> #> 1: HP:0000452 HP:0003986 0.003174386 #> 2: HP:0000452 HP:0004921 0.003211837 #> 3: HP:0000452 HP:0009655 0.003211837 #> 4: HP:0000452 HP:0031206 0.003114082 #> 5: HP:0000452 HP:0032411 0.003114082 #> 6: HP:0000452 HP:0034891 0.003114082

title: Ontologies
text: addition IC-based measures , bixverse implements Wang similarity. approach operates directly DAG structure ontology lets assign edge weight relationship type. ontologies multiple relationship types — Gene Ontology, instance — gives fine-grained control different edges contribute similarity. IC-based measures, can restrict computation subset terms:
code: # The HPO only has one relationship type; for the GO you would specify # several, e.g. c(is_a = 0.8, part_of = 0.6)  wang_weights <- setNames(0.8, "parent_of")  wang_similarity <- calculate_wang_sim_mat(   parent_child_dt = hpo_data,   weight = wang_weights )  wang_similarity[1:5, 1:5] # needs to be provided as previous chunk is not evaluated wang_weights <- setNames(0.8, "parent_of")  wang_similarity_subset <- calculate_wang_sim(   terms = random_term_sample,   parent_child_dt = hpo_data,   weight = wang_weights )  head(wang_similarity_subset) #>         term1      term2       sims #>        <char>     <char>      <num> #> 1: HP:0000452 HP:0003986 0.06945263 #> 2: HP:0000452 HP:0004921 0.11339664 #> 3: HP:0000452 HP:0009655 0.04711510 #> 4: HP:0000452 HP:0031206 0.06484329 #> 5: HP:0000452 HP:0032411 0.07044337 #> 6: HP:0000452 HP:0034891 0.12414544

title: Pathway activity functions
text: common question transcriptomics whether genes belonging given pathway concordantly - -regulated sample. Several algorithms tackle collapsing gene--sample expression matrix pathway--sample score matrix, widely used GSVA ssGSEA. biverse provides fast versions tricks hood make faster original implementations. Moreover, multiple contrasts (different drugs vs. DMSO example). bixverse also provides Rust-accelerated implementations mitch.

title: Pathway activity functions
text: Following GSVA vignette convention, generate synthetic expression data (Gaussian-distributed mimic normalised microarray RNA-seq log counts, Poisson-distributed mimic raw counts) together collection random gene sets.
code: p <- 10000L # genes n <- 100L # samples  # Gaussian expression matrix X <- matrix(   rnorm(p * n),   nrow = p,   dimnames = list(paste0("g", seq_len(p)), paste0("s", seq_len(n))) )  # Poisson count matrix X_counts <- matrix(   rpois(p * n, lambda = 10),   nrow = p,   dimnames = list(paste0("g", seq_len(p)), paste0("s", seq_len(n))) ) storage.mode(X_counts) <- "numeric"  # Random gene sets of varying size gs <- as.list(sample(10:100, size = 250, replace = TRUE)) gs <- lapply(   gs,   function(n, p) paste0("g", sample(seq_len(p), size = n, replace = FALSE)),   p ) names(gs) <- paste0("gs", seq_along(gs))

title: Pathway activity functions
text: Gaussian kernel version GSVA appropriate continuous expression values (log-CPM, microarray intensities, etc.). Running bixverse single call: original GSVA Bioconductor package available can verify results essentially identical. Minor differences numerical precision arise optimisations Rust side, per-pathway correlations two implementations consistently 0.99. Let’s compare speed (differences pronounced, computational fire power system .) count data Poisson kernel appropriate. change setting gaussian = FALSE: vs. original speed:
code: bixverse_res_gaussian <- calc_gsva(   exp = X,   pathways = gs,   gaussian = TRUE )  bixverse_res_gaussian[1:5, 1:5] #>              s1          s2          s3          s4           s5 #> gs1  0.17706400  0.14199980 -0.04229255  0.12585548  0.249232188 #> gs2 -0.21918302  0.36871659  0.23032289 -0.17733440  0.046289356 #> gs3 -0.03121185  0.02056382 -0.08304405  0.03617045 -0.129167990 #> gs4 -0.22610813 -0.14052022 -0.23308024  0.39597942 -0.005395304 #> gs5 -0.09591750  0.08546600  0.07149670 -0.08060380 -0.138726777 library(GSVA)  gsvaPar <- gsvaParam(X, gs) gsva_res_gaussian <- as.matrix(gsva(gsvaPar, verbose = FALSE))  correlations <- diag(cor(bixverse_res_gaussian, gsva_res_gaussian))  print(sprintf(   "All pathway correlations >= 0.99: %s (min: %.4f)",   all(correlations >= 0.99),   min(correlations) )) #> [1] "All pathway correlations >= 0.99: TRUE (min: 1.0000)" microbenchmark::microbenchmark(   gsva = {     gsvaPar <- gsvaParam(X, gs)     gsva(gsvaPar, verbose = FALSE)   },   bixverse = calc_gsva(exp = X, pathways = gs, gaussian = TRUE),   times = 3L ) #> Unit: milliseconds #>      expr       min        lq      mean    median        uq       max neval #>      gsva 2479.8985 2490.1909 2502.5672 2500.4834 2513.9016 2527.3199     3 #>  bixverse  578.7571  579.4475  580.1174  580.1379  580.7975  581.4572     3 bixverse_res_poisson <- calc_gsva(   exp = X_counts,   pathways = gs,   gaussian = FALSE )  bixverse_res_poisson[1:5, 1:5] #>              s1          s2          s3          s4          s5 #> gs1 -0.28201713 -0.10093545  0.11895674 -0.25479028  0.00557005 #> gs2  0.03700905  0.26620705  0.09808587  0.07601572 -0.10084857 #> gs3 -0.05559449 -0.21994194  0.07611016  0.04652204  0.02517169 #> gs4  0.05283749  0.10543643 -0.14106498  0.31842191  0.05903860 #> gs5  0.07994903  0.01416714 -0.02536083  0.00605250 -0.05586239 gsvaParPoisson <- gsvaParam(X_counts, gs, kcdf = "Poisson") gsva_res_poisson <- as.matrix(gsva(gsvaParPoisson, verbose = FALSE))  correlations <- diag(cor(bixverse_res_poisson, gsva_res_poisson))  print(sprintf(   "All pathway correlations >= 0.99: %s (min: %.4f)",   all(correlations >= 0.99),   min(correlations) )) #> [1] "All pathway correlations >= 0.99: TRUE (min: 1.0000)" microbenchmark::microbenchmark(   gsva = {     gsvaPar <- gsvaParam(X_counts, gs, kcdf = "Poisson")     gsva(gsvaPar, verbose = FALSE)   },   bixverse = calc_gsva(exp = X_counts, pathways = gs, gaussian = FALSE),   times = 3L ) #> Unit: seconds #>      expr       min        lq      mean title: Pathway activity functions
text: ssGSEA, first described Barbie et al., takes similar approach apply kernel-based normalisation across samples. bixverse provides Rust-optimised version well: Let’s compare GSVA version: check underlying speed differences:
code: bixverse_res_ssgsea <- calc_ssgsea(   exp = X,   pathways = gs )  bixverse_res_ssgsea[1:5, 1:5] #>               s1          s2         s3          s4        s5 #> gs1  0.213990800 0.097204998 0.03016442  0.13653622 0.2746750 #> gs2  0.003557942 0.236323629 0.20602659 -0.04633677 0.1894695 #> gs3  0.094623580 0.099010159 0.07491913  0.11798944 0.0243581 #> gs4 -0.021173777 0.007343977 0.02576545  0.31370460 0.1244642 #> gs5  0.066945162 0.164002624 0.12971064  0.03145801 0.1080797 ssgseaPar <- ssgseaParam(X, gs) ssgsea_res <- as.matrix(gsva(ssgseaPar, verbose = FALSE))  correlations <- diag(cor(bixverse_res_ssgsea, ssgsea_res))  print(sprintf(   "All pathway correlations >= 0.99: %s (min: %.4f)",   all(correlations >= 0.99),   min(correlations) )) #> [1] "All pathway correlations >= 0.99: TRUE (min: 1.0000)" microbenchmark::microbenchmark(   gsva = {     ssgseaPar <- ssgseaParam(X, gs)     gsva(ssgseaPar, verbose = FALSE)   },   bixverse = calc_ssgsea(exp = X, pathways = gs),   times = 3L ) #> Unit: milliseconds #>      expr      min       lq     mean   median       uq      max neval #>      gsva 604.3487 607.4562 610.1920 610.5637 613.1137 615.6637     3 #>  bixverse 117.0245 117.0266 117.0795 117.0287 117.1069 117.1852     3

title: Pathway activity functions
text: multiple contrasts - say, differential expression results several comparisons different omics layers - often want know pathways show coordinated changes across contrasts. mitch method exactly : ranks genes within contrast, computes mean ranks per pathway, uses MANOVA test identify pathways enriched one contrasts simultaneously. bixverse re-implements core algorithm slots workflow pathway activity functions… Also, heavily multi-threaded designed go brrrrrr terms speed. calc_mitch expects numeric matrix contrast statistics (genes rows, contrasts columns) named list gene sets. returns data.table containing per-pathway MANOVA p-values, FDR-adjusted p-values, individual contrast-level enrichment scores p-values. Note pathways smaller internal minimum set size dropped output, results sorted significance. function also validates input: passing matrix containing NA values throw error rather silently producing nonsense. original mitch package available can verify two implementations produce identical results. use example data bundled mitch: let’s check speed differences: GSVA ssGSEA implementations, observe meaningful speed improvements Rust, particularly number gene sets contrasts grows cores/oomph system .
code: set.seed(42L)  contrast_data <- matrix(rnorm(3 * 26), nrow = 26) colnames(contrast_data) <- sprintf("contrast_%i", 1:3) rownames(contrast_data) <- letters  gene_sets <- list(   pathway_A = sample(letters, 4),   pathway_B = sample(letters, 5),   pathway_C = sample(letters, 6),   pathway_D = sample(letters, 7) )  res <- calc_mitch(   contrast_mat = contrast_data,   gene_set_list = gene_sets )  res data(myImportedData, genesetsExample, package = "mitch")  mitch_res <- suppressMessages(mitch::mitch_calc(   myImportedData,   genesetsExample,   priority = "significance",   minsetsize = 5,   cores = 2 ))  bixverse_res <- calc_mitch(   contrast_mat = as.matrix(myImportedData),   gene_set_list = genesetsExample )  # Pathway ordering and FDR values should match exactly all(bixverse_res$pathway_names == mitch_res$enrichment_result$set) &&   all.equal(bixverse_res$manova_fdr, mitch_res$enrichment_result$p.adjustMANOVA) microbenchmark::microbenchmark(   mitch = suppressMessages(mitch::mitch_calc(     myImportedData,     genesetsExample,     priority = "significance",     minsetsize = 5,     cores = 2   )),   bixverse = calc_mitch(     contrast_mat = as.matrix(myImportedData),     gene_set_list = genesetsExample   ),   times = 5L )

title: Analysing PBMCs with bixverse
text: vignette walks standard single cell analysis PBMC3k data set using bixverse. read design choices introductory vignette, please first; vignette assumes familiarity SingleCells class, -disk storage cells--keep logic work.

title: Analysing PBMCs with bixverse
text: start downloading PBMC3k data set bundled package loading via Cell Ranger-style MTX /O. Let’s quick look variable table. column names MTX files always informative, rename gene symbol column something sensible set mappings Ensembl IDs symbols.
code: pbmc3k_path <- bixverse:::download_pbmc3k()  tempdir_pbmc <- tempdir()  sc_object <- SingleCells(dir_data = tempdir_pbmc)  mtx_io_params <- get_cell_ranger_params(pbmc3k_path)  sc_object <- load_mtx(   object = sc_object,   sc_mtx_io_param = mtx_io_params,   streaming = FALSE,   .verbose = TRUE ) #> Loading observations data from flat file into the DuckDB. #> Loading variable data from flat file into the DuckDB.  sc_object #> Single cell experiment (Single Cells). #>   No cells (original): 2700 #>    To keep n: 2700 #>   No genes: 11139 #>   HVG calculated: FALSE #>   PCA calculated: FALSE #>   Other embeddings: none #>   KNN generated: FALSE #>   SNN generated: FALSE var <- get_sc_var(sc_object)  head(var) #>    gene_idx         gene_id   column1 no_cells_exp #>       <num>          <char>    <char>        <int> #> 1:        1 ENSG00000225880 LINC00115           18 #> 2:        2 ENSG00000188976     NOC2L          258 #> 3:        3 ENSG00000188290      HES4          145 #> 4:        4 ENSG00000187608     ISG15         1206 #> 5:        5 ENSG00000131591  C1orf159           24 #> 6:        6 ENSG00000186891  TNFRSF18           92  setnames_sc(   object = sc_object,   table = "var",   old = "column1",   new = "gene_symbol" )  var <- get_sc_var(sc_object)  head(var) #>    gene_idx         gene_id gene_symbol no_cells_exp #>       <num>          <char>      <char>        <int> #> 1:        1 ENSG00000225880   LINC00115           18 #> 2:        2 ENSG00000188976       NOC2L          258 #> 3:        3 ENSG00000188290        HES4          145 #> 4:        4 ENSG00000187608       ISG15         1206 #> 5:        5 ENSG00000131591    C1orf159           24 #> 6:        6 ENSG00000186891    TNFRSF18           92  ensembl_to_symbol <- setNames(var$gene_symbol, var$gene_id) symbol_to_ensembl <- setNames(var$gene_id, var$gene_symbol)

title: Analysing PBMCs with bixverse
text: typical first step computing proportion counts mapping mitochondrial ribosomal genes per cell. added directly obs table DuckDB. can see now MT Ribo columns obs table. use per-cell QC metrics MAD-based outlier detection flag problematic cells. run_cell_qc function returns CellQc object carries metrics, per-metric outlier calls combined outlier vector. CellQc class plot method produces violin plots outliers highlighted.  store outlier flag obs table set cells keep. point , downstream methods (HVG selection, PCA, etc.) operate retained cells.
code: gs_of_interest <- list(   MT = var[grepl("^MT-", gene_symbol), gene_id],   Ribo = var[grepl("^RPS|^RPL", gene_symbol), gene_id] )  sc_object <- gene_set_proportions_sc(   sc_object,   gs_of_interest,   streaming = FALSE,   .verbose = TRUE )  sc_object[[1:5L]] #>    cell_idx          cell_id   nnz lib_size to_keep          MT      Ribo #>       <num>           <char> <num>    <num>  <lgcl>       <num>     <num> #> 1:        1 AAACATACAACCAC-1   778     2418    TRUE 0.030190241 0.4371381 #> 2:        2 AAACATTGAGCTAC-1  1346     4896    TRUE 0.037990198 0.4246323 #> 3:        3 AAACATTGATCAGC-1  1126     3144    TRUE 0.008905852 0.3171120 #> 4:        4 AAACCGTGCTTCCG-1   953     2632    TRUE 0.017477203 0.2431611 #> 5:        5 AAACCGTGTATGCG-1   520      979    TRUE 0.012257406 0.1491318 qc_df <- sc_object[[c("cell_id", "lib_size", "nnz", "MT")]]  metrics <- list(   log10_lib_size = log10(qc_df$lib_size),   log10_nnz = log10(qc_df$nnz),   MT = qc_df$MT )  directions <- c(   log10_lib_size = "twosided",   log10_nnz = "twosided",   MT = "above" )  qc <- run_cell_qc(metrics, directions, threshold = 3)  qc #> CellQc: 2700 cells, 537 outliers (19.9%) #> Metrics: #>   - log10_lib_size: 336 outliers [lower = 3.05, upper = 3.64] #>   - log10_nnz: 383 outliers [lower = 2.70, upper = 3.12] #>   - MT: 201 outliers [upper = 0.04] plots <- plot(qc, qc_df)  plots$log10_lib_size + plots$log10_nnz + plots$MT sc_object[["outlier"]] <- qc$combined  cells_to_keep <- qc_df[!qc$combined, cell_id]  sc_object <- set_cells_to_keep(sc_object, cells_to_keep)  sc_object #> Single cell experiment (Single Cells). #>   No cells (original): 2700 #>    To keep n: 2163 #>   No genes: 11139 #>   HVG calculated: FALSE #>   PCA calculated: FALSE #>   Other embeddings: none #>   KNN generated: FALSE #>   SNN generated: FALSE

title: Analysing PBMCs with bixverse
text: use per-cell QC metrics MAD-based outlier detection flag problematic cells. run_cell_qc function returns CellQc object carries metrics, per-metric outlier calls combined outlier vector. CellQc class plot method produces violin plots outliers highlighted.
code: qc_df <- sc_object[[c("cell_id", "lib_size", "nnz", "MT")]]  metrics <- list(   log10_lib_size = log10(qc_df$lib_size),   log10_nnz = log10(qc_df$nnz),   MT = qc_df$MT )  directions <- c(   log10_lib_size = "twosided",   log10_nnz = "twosided",   MT = "above" )  qc <- run_cell_qc(metrics, directions, threshold = 3)  qc #> CellQc: 2700 cells, 537 outliers (19.9%) #> Metrics: #>   - log10_lib_size: 336 outliers [lower = 3.05, upper = 3.64] #>   - log10_nnz: 383 outliers [lower = 2.70, upper = 3.12] #>   - MT: 201 outliers [upper = 0.04] plots <- plot(qc, qc_df)  plots$log10_lib_size + plots$log10_nnz + plots$MT

title: Analysing PBMCs with bixverse
text: store outlier flag obs table set cells keep. point , downstream methods (HVG selection, PCA, etc.) operate retained cells.
code: sc_object[["outlier"]] <- qc$combined  cells_to_keep <- qc_df[!qc$combined, cell_id]  sc_object <- set_cells_to_keep(sc_object, cells_to_keep)  sc_object #> Single cell experiment (Single Cells). #>   No cells (original): 2700 #>    To keep n: 2163 #>   No genes: 11139 #>   HVG calculated: FALSE #>   PCA calculated: FALSE #>   Other embeddings: none #>   KNN generated: FALSE #>   SNN generated: FALSE

title: Analysing PBMCs with bixverse
text: QC done, move standard pipeline: highly variable gene selection, PCA, nearest neighbour computation.
code: sc_object <- find_hvg_sc(   object = sc_object,   hvg_no = 2000L,   .verbose = TRUE )  sc_object <- calculate_pca_sc(   object = sc_object,   no_pcs = 30L,   sparse_svd = TRUE ) #> Using sparse SVD solving on scaled data on 2000 HVG.  # the data is so tiny that exhaustive kNN search is faster than building # an approximate nearest neighbour index sc_object <- find_neighbours_sc(   object = sc_object,   neighbours_params = params_sc_neighbours(     knn = list(knn_method = "exhaustive")   ) ) #>  #> Generating sNN graph (full: FALSE). #> Transforming sNN data to igraph.

title: Analysing PBMCs with bixverse
text: Leiden clustering followed differential gene expression across clusters.
code: sc_object <- find_clusters_sc(sc_object, res = 1.5, name = "leiden_clusters")  all_markers <- find_all_markers_sc(   object = sc_object,   column_of_interest = "leiden_clusters" ) #> Processing group 1 out of 8. #> Processing group 2 out of 8. #> Processing group 3 out of 8. #> Processing group 4 out of 8. #> Processing group 5 out of 8. #> Processing group 6 out of 8. #> Processing group 7 out of 8. #> Processing group 8 out of 8.  all_markers[, gene_symbol := ensembl_to_symbol[gene_id]]  head(all_markers[fdr <= 0.05][order(-abs(lfc))]) #>      grp         gene_id      lfc     prop1      prop2 z_scores      p_values #>    <num>          <char>    <num>     <num>      <num>    <num>         <num> #> 1:     6 ENSG00000163220 4.174770 0.9887324 0.20077434 29.09827 1.887722e-186 #> 2:     8 ENSG00000105374 4.156168 1.0000000 0.25049117 18.50890  8.752290e-77 #> 3:     6 ENSG00000090382 4.081069 1.0000000 0.51050884 29.66810 9.907717e-194 #> 4:     8 ENSG00000115523 3.934315 0.9527559 0.13654225 17.52567  4.562691e-69 #> 5:     6 ENSG00000143546 3.663987 0.9718310 0.11504425 28.62794 1.508612e-180 #> 6:     8 ENSG00000100453 3.544770 0.9842520 0.06974459 18.39899  6.691597e-76 #>              fdr gene_symbol #>            <num>      <char> #> 1: 4.275690e-183      S100A9 #> 2:  3.963037e-73        NKG7 #> 3: 4.488196e-190         LYZ #> 4:  5.164966e-66        GNLY #> 5: 2.278005e-177      S100A8 #> 6:  1.514978e-72        GZMB

title: Analysing PBMCs with bixverse
text: Let us run quickly UMAP tSNE. can pull embeddings back data.tables plot (longer term, idea provide plotting helpers bixverse.plots). obs columns can appended directly.  Let’s check tSNE  Dot plots standard way visualise marker gene expression across clusters. extract_dot_plot_data method handles Rust-level extraction, grouping statistics optional min-max scaling one call.  feature plots per-cell visualisations, extract_gene_expression returns long data.table one row per cell columns requested gene, plus obs metadata need.
code: sc_object <- umap_sc(sc_object, knn_method = "annoy") #> Running UMAP. #> Using n_epochs = 500 (dataset <10k samples or adam_parallel optimiser) #> Using provided kNN graph.  sc_object <- tsne_sc(sc_object, perplexity = 30, knn_method = "annoy") #> Running t-SNE. #> Using provided kNN graph. umap_dt <- as.data.table(   get_embedding(sc_object, "umap"),   keep.rownames = "cell_id" )[, leiden_clusters := sc_object[["leiden_clusters"]]]  ggplot(umap_dt, aes(x = umap_1, y = umap_2)) +   geom_point(aes(colour = as.factor(leiden_clusters)), size = 0.5) +   theme_bw() +   labs(colour = "Leiden cluster") tsne_dt <- as.data.table(   get_embedding(sc_object, "tsne"),   keep.rownames = "cell_id" )[, leiden_clusters := sc_object[["leiden_clusters"]]]  ggplot(tsne_dt, aes(x = tsne_1, y = tsne_2)) +   geom_point(aes(colour = as.factor(leiden_clusters)), size = 0.5) +   theme_bw() +   labs(colour = "Leiden cluster") cell_markers <- c(   "MS4A1",   "CD79B",   "GNLY",   "CD3D",   "CD8A",   "CD4",   "CD14",   "FCER1A",   "FCGR3A",   "LYZ",   "PPBP" )  features <- symbol_to_ensembl[cell_markers]  dot_dt <- extract_dot_plot_data(   object = sc_object,   features = features,   grouping_variable = "leiden_clusters",   scale_exp = TRUE )  dot_dt[, gene_symbol := ensembl_to_symbol[as.character(gene)]] dot_dt[, gene_symbol := factor(gene_symbol, levels = rev(cell_markers))]  ggplot(dot_dt, aes(x = group, y = gene_symbol)) +   geom_point(aes(size = pct_exp, colour = scaled_exp)) +   scale_colour_viridis_c() +   scale_size_continuous(range = c(0, 6)) +   theme_bw() +   labs(     size = "% expressed",     colour = "Scaled\nexpression",     x = "Cluster",     y = ""   ) expr_dt <- extract_gene_expression(   object = sc_object,   features = symbol_to_ensembl[c("CD14", "MS4A1", "CD3E")],   obs_cols = "leiden_clusters" )  # attach UMAP coordinates umap_embd <- as.data.table(   get_embedding(sc_object, "umap"),   keep.rownames = "cell_id" )  plot_dt <- merge(expr_dt, umap_embd, by = "cell_id")  # melt to long for faceting gene_cols <- symbol_to_ensembl[c("CD14", "MS4A1", "CD3E")]  plot_long <- melt(   plot_dt,   id.vars = c("cell_id", "umap_1", "umap_2"),   measure.vars = gene_cols,   variable.name = "gene",   value.name = "expression" ) plot_long[, gene_symbol := ensembl_to_symbol[as.character(gene)]]  ggplot(plot_long, aes(x = umap_1, y = umap_2)) +   geom_point(aes(colour = expression), size = 0.3) +   scale_colour_viridis_c() +   facet_wrap(~gene_symbol) +   theme_bw() +   labs(colour = "log1p\nexpression")

code: sc_object <- umap_sc(sc_object, knn_method = "annoy") #> Running UMAP. #> Using n_epochs = 500 (dataset <10k samples or adam_parallel optimiser) #> Using provided kNN graph.  sc_object <- tsne_sc(sc_object, perplexity = 30, knn_method = "annoy") #> Running t-SNE. #> Using provided kNN graph. umap_dt <- as.data.table(   get_embedding(sc_object, "umap"),   keep.rownames = "cell_id" )[, leiden_clusters := sc_object[["leiden_clusters"]]]  ggplot(umap_dt, aes(x = umap_1, y = umap_2)) +   geom_point(aes(colour = as.factor(leiden_clusters)), size = 0.5) +   theme_bw() +   labs(colour = "Leiden cluster") tsne_dt <- as.data.table(   get_embedding(sc_object, "tsne"),   keep.rownames = "cell_id" )[, leiden_clusters := sc_object[["leiden_clusters"]]]  ggplot(tsne_dt, aes(x = tsne_1, y = tsne_2)) +   geom_point(aes(colour = as.factor(leiden_clusters)), size = 0.5) +   theme_bw() +   labs(colour = "Leiden cluster")

title: Analysing PBMCs with bixverse
text: Dot plots standard way visualise marker gene expression across clusters. extract_dot_plot_data method handles Rust-level extraction, grouping statistics optional min-max scaling one call.
code: cell_markers <- c(   "MS4A1",   "CD79B",   "GNLY",   "CD3D",   "CD8A",   "CD4",   "CD14",   "FCER1A",   "FCGR3A",   "LYZ",   "PPBP" )  features <- symbol_to_ensembl[cell_markers]  dot_dt <- extract_dot_plot_data(   object = sc_object,   features = features,   grouping_variable = "leiden_clusters",   scale_exp = TRUE )  dot_dt[, gene_symbol := ensembl_to_symbol[as.character(gene)]] dot_dt[, gene_symbol := factor(gene_symbol, levels = rev(cell_markers))]  ggplot(dot_dt, aes(x = group, y = gene_symbol)) +   geom_point(aes(size = pct_exp, colour = scaled_exp)) +   scale_colour_viridis_c() +   scale_size_continuous(range = c(0, 6)) +   theme_bw() +   labs(     size = "% expressed",     colour = "Scaled\nexpression",     x = "Cluster",     y = ""   )

title: Analysing PBMCs with bixverse
text: feature plots per-cell visualisations, extract_gene_expression returns long data.table one row per cell columns requested gene, plus obs metadata need.
code: expr_dt <- extract_gene_expression(   object = sc_object,   features = symbol_to_ensembl[c("CD14", "MS4A1", "CD3E")],   obs_cols = "leiden_clusters" )  # attach UMAP coordinates umap_embd <- as.data.table(   get_embedding(sc_object, "umap"),   keep.rownames = "cell_id" )  plot_dt <- merge(expr_dt, umap_embd, by = "cell_id")  # melt to long for faceting gene_cols <- symbol_to_ensembl[c("CD14", "MS4A1", "CD3E")]  plot_long <- melt(   plot_dt,   id.vars = c("cell_id", "umap_1", "umap_2"),   measure.vars = gene_cols,   variable.name = "gene",   value.name = "expression" ) plot_long[, gene_symbol := ensembl_to_symbol[as.character(gene)]]  ggplot(plot_long, aes(x = umap_1, y = umap_2)) +   geom_point(aes(colour = expression), size = 0.3) +   scale_colour_viridis_c() +   facet_wrap(~gene_symbol) +   theme_bw() +   labs(colour = "log1p\nexpression")

what: Using the Rust functions in more general R code
title: Bixverse R <> Rust interface
text: bixverse exposes number R/Rust functions useful beyond higher-level package functionality. vignette covers: Correlations (via rs_cor()). Covariance (via rs_covariance()). Covariance correlation (via rs_cov2cor()). Distance calculations (via rs_dist()). Correlations two matrices (via rs_cor2()). Mutual information columns (via rs_mutual_information()). Set similarities (via rs_set_similarity_list() rs_set_similarity()). may useful package development. Note vignette built GitHub runner 2-core machine ~8 GB memory. performance differences tend (even) pronounced machines cores computational power.

title: Bixverse R <> Rust interface
text: function prefixed rs_ calls directly Rust can panic misused — check inputs passing . LOT functions package, exported. ? want people able integrate code R packages, feel brave, can just use Rust crate directly, see . Let’s check speed. ranked correlations? speed Covariance matrices also common… speed comparisons Rust implementation shows least improvement — operation already cheap base R — interface remains consistent rest rs_ family. far, functions pairwise column-based correlations. two correlations ? Let’s compare R: speed: Note dist() computes row-wise distances, rs_dist() expects columns represent observations — hence transpose. speed… speed… ? benchmarks … Rust version defaults sqrt(nrow()) bins n_bins = NULL. Two discretisation strategies available. Equal width: Equal frequency: bixverse also exposes Rust-accelerated set similarity calculations. illustrate performance, comparison naive R implementation Jaccard similarity across two lists. Starting sequential purrr approach baseline: Parallelising via furrr gives meaningful speedup: mirai reduces process scheduling overhead (somtimes) compared future bit better: Depending system optimised parallel R version may around 4x faster naive sequential baseline. Now compare Rust implementation: margin subtle. functions shown vignette representative sample exposed bixverse: many specialised functions throughout package worth exploring (brave can deal panic ).
code: library(bixverse) no_rows <- 500L no_cols <- 500L  set.seed(10101L)  random_data <- matrix(   data = rnorm(no_rows * no_cols),   ncol = no_cols,   nrow = no_rows ) r_pearsons_res <- cor(random_data)  rust_pearsons_res <- rs_cor(random_data, spearman = FALSE)  # tiny numerical differences do exist all.equal(r_pearsons_res, rust_pearsons_res, tolerance = 1e-15) #> [1] TRUE microbenchmark::microbenchmark(   r = cor(random_data),   rust = rs_cor(random_data, spearman = FALSE),   times = 10L ) #> Unit: milliseconds #>  expr       min         lq       mean     median         uq        max neval #>     r 135.23978 135.403175 135.610917 135.430872 135.476443 136.889741    10 #>  rust   5.36159   5.446449   6.324538   5.780993   7.337089   8.298754    10 r_spearman_res <- cor(random_data, method = "spearman")  rust_spearman_res <- rs_cor(random_data, spearman = TRUE)  all.equal(r_spearman_res, rust_spearman_res, tolerance = 1e-15) #> [1] TRUE microbenchmark::microbenchmark(   r = cor(random_data, method = "spearman"),   rust = rs_cor(random_data, spearman = TRUE),   times = 10L ) #> Unit: milliseconds #>  expr        min         lq       mean     median         uq       max neval #>     r 166.829258 167.166397 169.001063 168.952673 170.063296 173.01536    10 #>  rust   7.162844   7.368717   8.372765   7.634873   9.392846  11.02051    10 r_covar_res <- cov(random_data)  rust_covar_res <- rs_covariance(random_data)  all.equal(r_covar_res, rust_covar_res, tolerance = 1e-15) #> [1] TRUE microbenchmark::microbenchmark(   r = cov(random_data),   rust = rs_covariance(random_data),   times = 10L ) #> Unit: milliseconds #>  expr        min         lq       mean     median         uq        max neval #>     r 135.035249 135.085934 135.376455 135.118925 135.320181 137.234906    10 #>  rust   4.206664   4.904427   5.655972   5.178429   5.808425   8.734257    10 r_cor_from_covar <- cov2cor(r_covar_res)  rust_cor_from_covar <- rs_cov2cor(rust_covar_res)  all.equal(r_cor_from_covar, rust_cor_from_covar, tolerance = 1e-15) #> [1] TRUE microbenchmark::microbenchmark(   r = cov2cor(r_covar_res),   rust = rs_cov2cor(rust_covar_res),   times = 10L ) #> Unit: milliseconds #>  expr      min       lq     mean   median       uq      max neval #>     r 2.260721 2.463168 3.425157 3.212904 3.984319 6.046819    10 #>  rust 1.454015 1.680818 2.869126 2.043781 3.580155 7.095086    10 no_rows_2 <- 500L no_cols_2 <- 400L  set.seed(23L)  random_data_2 <- matrix(   data = rnorm(no_rows_2 * no_cols_2, mean = 2, sd = 2),   ncol = no_cols_2,   nrow = no_rows_2 ) r_cor_2_matrices <- cor(random_data, random_data_2)  rust_cor_2_matrices <- rs_cor2(random_data, random_data_2, spearman = FALSE)  # small precision differences are further propagated here all.equal(r_cor_2_matrices, rust_cor_2_matrices, tolerance = 1e-14) #> [1] TRUE microbenchmark::microbenchmark(   r = cor(random_data, random_data_2),   rust = rs_cor2(random_data, random_data_2, spearman = FALSE),   times = 10L ) #> Unit: milliseconds #>  expr        min         lq       mean     median         uq        max neval #>     r 190.752101 190.808167 191.067043 190.828919 190.955832 193.066051    10 #>  rust   4.788851   5.349508   6.170928   5.853708   6.912124   8.194348    10 r_euclidean_distance <- as.matrix(   dist(random_data, method = "euclidean") )  rs_euclidean_distance <- rs_dist(   t(random_data),   distance_type = "euclidean" )  all.equal(   r_euclidean_distance,   rs_euclidean_distance,   tolerance = 1e-14,   check.attributes = FALSE ) #> [1] TRUE microbenchmark::microbenchmark(   r = dist(random_data, method = "euclidean"),   rust = rs_dist(random_data, distance_type = "euclidean"),   times = 10L ) #> Unit: milliseconds #>  expr       min        lq      mean    median        uq       max neval #>     r 96.073298 96.165650 96.390316 96.201391 96.252953 98.025402    10 #>  rust  4.486005  4.512726  4.821457  4.789312  5.132733  5.219905    10 r_manhattan_distance <- as.matrix(   dist(random_data, method = "manhattan") )  rs_manhattan_distance <- rs_dist(   t(random_data),   distance_type = "manhattan" )  all.equal(   r_manhattan_distance,   rs_manhattan_distance,   tolerance = 1e-14,   check.attributes = FALSE ) #> [1] TRUE microbenchmark::microbenchmark(   r = dist(random_data, method = "manhattan"),   rust = rs_dist(random_data, distance_type = "manhattan"),   times = 10L ) #> Unit: milliseconds #>  expr      min       lq     mean   median       uq      max neval #>     r 91.09046 91.46613 91.60626 91.54609 91.65665 92.72860    10 #>  rust 17.13978 17.18915 17.44114 17.46048 17.56030 17.77447    10 r_canberra_distance <- as.matrix(   dist(random_data, method = "canberra") )  rs_canberra_distance <- rs_dist(   t(random_data),   distance_type = "canberra" )  all.equal(   r_canberra_distance,   rs_canberra_distance,   tolerance = 1e-14,   check.attributes = FALSE ) #> [1] TRUE microbenchmark::microbenchmark(   r = dist(random_data, method = "canberra"),   rust = rs_dist(random_data, distance_type = "canberra"),   times = 10L ) #> Unit: milliseconds #>  expr       min        lq      mean    median        uq       max neval #>     r 137.17513 137.20020 137.53812 137.31642 137.45189 139.59654    10 #>  rust  45.92332  46.01331  46.16769  46.10759  46.24756  46.65721    10 set.seed(246L)  nrows <- 100 ncols <- 100  mat <- matrix(data = rnorm(nrows * ncols), nrow = nrows, ncol = ncols) rownames(mat) <- sprintf("sample_%i", 1:nrows) colnames(mat) <- sprintf("feature_%i", 1:ncols) rust_res_mi <- rs_mutual_info(   mat,   n_bins = NULL,   normalise = FALSE,   strategy = "equal_width" ) rownames(rust_res_mi) <- colnames(rust_res_mi) <- colnames(mat)  infotheo_res_mi <- infotheo::mutinformation(infotheo::discretize(   mat,   disc = "equalwidth",   nbins = sqrt(nrow(mat)) ))  all.equal(rust_res_mi, infotheo_res_mi) #> [1] TRUE microbenchmark::microbenchmark(   infotheo = infotheo::mutinformation(infotheo::discretize(     mat,     disc = "equalwidth",     nbins = sqrt(nrow(mat))   )),   rust = rs_mutual_info(     mat,     n_bins = NULL,     normalise = FALSE,     strategy = "equal_width"   ),   times = 10L ) #> Unit: milliseconds #>      expr       min        lq      mean    median        uq      max neval #>  infotheo 83.969600 84.056712 84.733703 84.310877 85.888663 86.43741    10 #>      rust  3.426477  3.501668  3.633614  3.642951  3.730765  3.82993    10 rust_res_mi <- rs_mutual_info(   mat,   n_bins = NULL,   normalise = FALSE,   strategy = "equal_freq" ) rownames(rust_res_mi) <- colnames(rust_res_mi) <- colnames(mat)  infotheo_res_mi <- infotheo::mutinformation(infotheo::discretize(   mat,   disc = "equalfreq",   nbins = sqrt(nrow(mat)) ))  all.equal(rust_res_mi, infotheo_res_mi) #> [1] TRUE microbenchmark::microbenchmark(   infotheo = infotheo::mutinformation(infotheo::discretize(     mat,     disc = "equalfreq",     nbins = sqrt(nrow(mat))   )),   rust = rs_mutual_info(     mat,     n_bins = NULL,     normalise = FALSE,     strategy = "equal_freq"   ),   times = 10L ) #> Unit: milliseconds #>      expr        min        lq       mean     median         uq        max #>  infotheo 100.844355 101.57822 102.020389 101.896930 102.374662 103.311671 #>      rust   4.417227   4.51546   4.633386   4.655677   4.723369   4.810441 #>  neval #>     10 #>     10 jaccard_sim <- function(x, y) {   length(intersect(x, y)) / length(union(x, y)) }  set.seed(42L)  random_sets_1 <- purrr::map(   1:1000L,   ~ paste(sample(LETTERS, sample(1:20, 1))) )  random_sets_2 <- purrr::map(   1:500L,   ~ paste(sample(LETTERS, sample(1:20, 1))) ) tictoc::tic()  r_results <- purrr::map(   random_sets_1,   \(x) {     purrr::map_dbl(random_sets_2, \(y) jaccard_sim(x, y))   },   .progress = TRUE ) #>  ■■■                                7% |  ETA: 22s #>  ■■■■■■■                           20% |  ETA: 20s #>  ■■■■■■■■■■■                       32% |  ETA: 16s #>  ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■                   45% |  ETA: 13s #>  ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■                57% |  ETA: 10s #>  ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■            70% |  ETA:  7s #>  ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■        83% |  ETA:  4s #>  ■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■■    95% |  ETA:  1s  similarity_matrix <- matrix(   data = unlist(r_results),   nrow = length(random_sets_1),   ncol = length(random_sets_2),   byrow = TRUE )  tictoc::toc() #> 23.971 sec elapsed future::plan(strategy = future::multisession(workers = parallel::detectCores()))  tictoc::tic()  r_results <- furrr::future_map(   random_sets_1,   \(x) {     purrr::map_dbl(random_sets_2, \(y) jaccard_sim(x, y))   },   .progress = TRUE )  similarity_matrix <- matrix(   data = unlist(r_results),   nrow = length(random_sets_1),   ncol = length(random_sets_2),   byrow = TRUE )  tictoc::toc() #> 13.853 sec elapsed  future::plan(strategy = future::sequential()) mirai::daemons(parallel::detectCores())  tictoc::tic()  r_results_mirai <- mirai::mirai_map(   random_sets_1,   \(x, sets_2) {     purrr::map_dbl(sets_2, \(y) {       length(intersect(x, y)) / length(union(x, y))     })   },   .args = list(sets_2 = random_sets_2) )[]  similarity_matrix <- matrix(   data = unlist(r_results_mirai),   nrow = length(random_sets_1),   ncol = length(random_sets_2),   byrow = TRUE )  tictoc::toc() #> 13.161 sec elapsed  mirai::daemons(0) tictoc::tic()  rust_res <- rs_set_similarity_list2(   s_1_list = random_sets_1,   s_2_list = infotheo_res_mi <- infotheo::mutinformation(infotheo::discretize(   mat,   disc = "equalwidth",   nbins = sqrt(nrow(mat)) ))  all.equal(rust_res_mi, infotheo_res_mi) #> [1] TRUE n_bins = NULL,     normalise = FALSE,     strategy = "equal_width"   ),   times = 10L ) #> Unit: milliseconds #>      expr       min        lq      mean    median        uq      max neval #>  infotheo
